title: The Dungeon Dive's Top 50 Games of All Time* - part 1
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KOF8EQ_YcOU

Daniel (@dungeondive) opens the unscientific top-50 with a confession: he ran 200 dungeon-crawl and adventure games through the Pub Meeple comparison engine in a single sitting, took the top 50, and is rolling them out ten at a time. The cutoff at 50 is honest — anything below would just be more games he likes — and the rankings are framed as a snapshot, not a verdict.

Numbers 50 to 41: **Runebound 3rd Edition** (with Unbreakable Bonds — solid co-op threat management, the pog combat is at least unique), **Dungeoneer: Tomb of the Lich Lord** (a 2003 competitive crawl with a clever glory/peril economy), **Aftermath** (Watership Down meets dungeon crawl — charming world, terrible rulebook), **Fallout** (with the Atomic Bonds expansion to fix the drab base game), **Shadows of Malice** (abstract, demands player imagination), **LotR: Journeys in Middle-earth** (great app, too many tiles), **Advanced HeroQuest** (best dungeon-toolbox rules, but the box is famously paltry — better treated as a HeroQuest expansion), **Assault on Doomrock** (terrific combat, way too long), **Tomb** (an abundance of art and ambition that the design never quite earns — Daniel keeps it on potential alone), and **Warhammer Quest: Blackstone Fortress** (GW's most-supported specialty box, lacklustre character progression).

----

Tomb is the controversial entry — included for what it could be rather than what it is. Is there a game in your collection you keep around for its potential, hoping someone (you, the designer, the fan community) eventually cracks it?
